title: Understanding Multi-Agent LLM Frameworks
subtitle: Orogat, Rostam & Mansour propose the first unified benchmark comparing how multi-agent LLM frameworks structure interaction, memory, and coordination — and how each axis moves downstream task performance.

blocks.label: TL;DR
blocks.text: Architecture choice (hierarchical vs peer-to-peer) affects task pass rate **more than model choice**. Memory persistence across turns is the #1 predictor of multi-step task success. LLM-as-judge evaluation has high variance without task-conditioned rubrics.

blocks.markdown:
## Why this matters for MAE eval

This is the closest published cousin of what **MAE** does. The paper's three evaluation axes — *agent interaction*, *information storage*, *task coordination* — map almost 1-to-1 onto what your 29 evaluators try to score. The framing alone is worth stealing for any eval PR you propose to Jun.

If you pitch *one* eval PR this week, make it a **task-conditioned rubric** experiment — the paper shows judge variance drops dramatically when rubrics are conditioned on task type rather than generic quality dimensions.

## The three axes

| Axis | What it measures | MAE equivalent (best guess) |
|---|---|---|
| Agent interaction | Who talks to whom, with what protocol | Edge cases in multi-turn edit loops |
| Information storage | How state is persisted across turns | Memory-based evaluators |
| Task coordination | Planning + delegation semantics | Rule-based evaluators for plan adherence |

## Key findings, annotated

1. **Hierarchical architectures beat peer-to-peer** on tasks with clear delegation structure, but lose on open-ended exploratory tasks. MAE's video-editing loop looks hierarchical (planner → editors) — so the paper's hierarchical results are the relevant baseline.
2. **Memory persistence > model size** above a certain threshold. Once you're past a mid-sized model, adding *any* durable memory improves multi-step success more than upgrading GPT-4→GPT-4-Turbo-equivalent.
3. **Judge variance reduction** — task-conditioned rubrics cut inter-judge disagreement by ~40% vs generic "helpful/honest/harmless"-style scorecards.

blocks.text: Evaluation is the only place where architecture choices are forced to be honest.

blocks.markdown:
## What I'd do with this by Friday

- Map each of the 29 MAE evaluators to one of the paper's 3 axes. Fill in the matrix — gaps become PR candidates.
- Pick **one** task type in MAE and draft a task-conditioned rubric. Compare judge variance to the existing generic rubric on 20 cases.
- Share the axis mapping with Jun as a pre-read before your next 1:1.

items: Map 29 MAE evaluators to paper's 3 axes
Draft task-conditioned rubric for one MAE task type

blocks.text: Open the PDF, set a 30-minute timer for the first pass, come back and tick the first three boxes above.
